title: Aces

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-04-probability-theory.md

setup.narrative: A standard 52-card deck is dealt evenly to 4 players, 13 cards each.
rules: There are exactly 4 aces in the deck.
setup.question: What is the probability that EACH of the 4 players receives exactly one ace?

learningObjectives: Use the multiplication rule on conditional events.
Compute P(each new ace lands in a fresh pile | the previous ones did).
Recognize the running fraction of remaining 'fresh pile' slots.

stages.label: Choose your technique
stages.prompt: How do you compute P(each player gets exactly one ace)?

options.text: Multiplication rule: P(ace 1 in some pile)*P(ace 2 in different pile|ace 1 placed)*P(ace 3 in 3rd pile|aces 1,2 placed)*P(ace 4 in 4th pile|aces 1,2,3 placed).
Direct combinatorial count of all cases.

stages.explanation: Treat the aces sequentially. Each ace has 51, 50, 49 remaining card slots after the prior aces are placed. We need each new ace to land in a DIFFERENT pile from the previous ones.

stages.label: Compute the four conditional probabilities
stages.prompt: Place the aces one by one. P(ace_1 in some pile) = 1 (trivially). P(ace_2 in a different pile | ace_1 placed) = ?

options.text: 39/51.  After ace_1 takes one of 13 slots in some pile, 51 cards remain; 39 of them belong to the OTHER three piles.

stages.explanation: Of the 51 unseen card-slots after ace_1 is placed, 12 are in ace_1's pile and 39 are in the other three piles (3 * 13 = 39).

stages.label: Continue the chain
stages.prompt: P(ace_3 in a different pile from aces 1, 2 | aces 1, 2 placed in 2 distinct piles)?  And P(ace_4 in the last unused pile | aces 1, 2, 3 in distinct piles)?

options.text: 26/50 (ace_3) and 13/49 (ace_4).  After 2 aces in 2 piles, 50 slots remain with 26 in the OTHER 2 piles. After 3 aces in 3 piles, 49 slots remain with 13 in the LAST pile.

stages.explanation: After 2 aces placed: 13 + 13 = 26 slots in the OTHER 2 piles, out of 50 remaining cards. After 3 aces placed: 13 slots in the LAST pile, out of 49 remaining cards.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Verify by direct combinatorial counting: C(13,1)^4 * (52!/(13!)^4) / total_deals.
What's P(one player gets all 4 aces)?
What if the deck has 5 aces and we deal 5 piles of 10 each?
